caption: Fig. 1. Phase diagram of Fe–20Mn–Al–C system at 1 100°C with estimated iso- ${ \cdot } D _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { f c c } }$ contour lines.
paper_abstract: Mechanical properties of Fe–20Mn–(10–14)Al–(0–1.8)C (mass%) quaternary and Fe–20Mn–(10–14)Al– (0.75–1.8)C–5Cr (mass%) quinary alloys were investigated by hardness, cold-workability and tensile tests at room temperature. The g(fcc) alloys in both quaternary and quinary systems with a low density of less than 7.0 g/cm3 showed an excellent ductility and their hardness and tensile strength increased with increasing Al and C contents. The gume fraction. TEM observation confirmed that high hardness and tensile strength of the alloys with high Al and C contents are caused by the precipitation of nano-size k-carbide with perovskite structure during cooling from the annealing temperature. Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr alloy with a density of 6.51 g/cm3 showed a high specific strength of more than 180 MPa · cm3 /g with a good tensile elongation of 40%. The present Fe– 20Mn–Al–C(–5Cr) alloys showed a higher specific strength than conventional steels.
paper_title: High-strength Fe–20Mn–Al–C-based Alloys with Low Density

citation_sentences: Fig. 3(b) exhibited a high hardness of 431 Hv with a good cold-workability of 71%. This means that k-carbide is suppressed by the addition of Cr. In addition, the Fe–20Mn– 11Al–1.8C–5Cr g alloy as well as the Fe–20Mn–10Al– 1.5C quaternary g alloy showed an upper yield point followed by almost constant flow stress. The formation of a certain amount of k phase as shown in Fig. 3(c) caused a drastic decrement of cold-workability. As with the quaternary y+α alloys, the hardness of quinary γ+α alloys increased with increasing Al and C contents. The quinary y+α alloys with Al content of 13 mass%, which possesses a low $D _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { f c c } }$ below $6 . 5 \mathrm { g } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 3 }$ , exhibited high strength and relatively large elongation. Therefore, even in the ghigh strength, together with good ductility, can be obtained by controlling the a volume fraction.

The specific strengths of all specimens are listed in Table 1. Fe–20Mn–Al–C(–5Cr) alloys with Al content below 13 mass% and C content over 1.0 mass% showed a high specific strength of over $1 2 0 \mathrm { M P a } \cdot \mathrm { c m } ^ { 3 } / \mathrm { g } ,$ and especially, the Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr alloy with $D _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { f c c } } { = } 6 . 5 1 \mathrm { g } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 3 }$ exhibited a high specific strength of over 180 MPa · cm3 /g with good tensile elongation of around 30%. It was confirmed by X-ray measurement that there was no stress-induced martensite phase in the tensile deformed specimen.36) The reason why the Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr g alloy with the microstructure shown in Fig. 3(c) showed high hardness, yield strength and tensile strength without the large loss in elongation should be due to the precipitation of a very fine K phase during quenching.Ishida et al.have reported that in Fe–20Mn–10.5Al–1.2C alloy, the precipitation of a very fine k phase occurs during quenching from temperatures above $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } . ^ { 3 4 ) }$ It has also been reported that aging of Fe–Mn–Al–C alloys with a g phase produces the precipitation of the k phase, which leads to age hardening.16,37–39) Therefore, in this study, the dependence of the cooling rate after the annealing treatment on the microstructure and the mechanical properties in the Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr g alloy was investigated.

citation_sentences: Figure 5(a) shows the stress–strain curves obtained in the Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr g alloy quenched in water and air cooled to room temperature from the annealing temperature of $1 1 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , where the cooling rates of the water quenching and the air cooling were about $1 1 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } / \mathrm { s }$ and about $1 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } / \mathbf { s } ,$ , respectively. The air-cooled specimen had a much higher yield strength than the water-quenched specimen and showed an upper yield point followed by almost constant flow stress. Such stress–strain behaviors are quite similar to those of the Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C quaternary and the Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr quinary g alloys quenched in water, as shown in Fig. 4. Figure 5(b) shows the true stress–strain curves of Fig. 5(a). The water-quenched specimen showed an almost linear work-hardening, while the aircooled specimen showed an almost zero work-hardening rate at a small plastic strain region below 5 % and then work-hardening with increasing plastic strain. Figures 6(a) and 6(b) show the diffraction patterns taken from the waterquenched and the air-cooled Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr alloys, respectively. Although both patterns reveal extra spots between the fundamental reflections, which should indicate the formation of the k phase with the perovskite structure, the intensity of the superlattice reflections in the air-cooled specimen is much higher than that in the water-quenched specimen. Figures 6(c) and 6(d) show dark field images of the (100) superlattice reflection in the water-quenched and air-cooled Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr alloys, respectively. In the water-quenched specimen, very fine k phase precipitates are slightly confirmed, while the cuboidal k phase precipitates aligned in the -001 directions are clearly observed in the air-cooled specimen. The microstructure observed in the air-cooled Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr alloy is very similar to the morphology observed in Ni-based superalloys with the $\gamma { + } \gamma ^ { \prime }$ two-phase.Therefore, it is concluded that high hardness and strength, as well as a small work-hardening rate obtained in the water-quenched Fe–Mn–Al–C(–Cr) alloys with a high C content are due to the nano-size precipitation of k phase during cooling from the annealing temperature. The formation mechanism of very fine k precipitates during cooling is not clear at present, even though one could possibly attribute it to precipitation through spinodal decomposition from the point of view of the crystal structure similarity between the g (fcc) and the k (perovskite) phases. It has been reported that composition fluctuation due to spinodal decomposition precedes the precipitation of the k-carbide in aged Fe–(30–33)Mn–(8–9)Al–

citation_sentences: at 1 000°C and 900°C followed by water quenching (WQ) and air cooling (AC) are shown in Table 3. It is seen that the yield and tensile strengths increased and the elongation decreased with decreasing annealing temperature, which is considered to be due to the formation of a, k and Cr-carbide phases with decreasing annealing temperature. Figure 7(a) shows the plots of the tensile strength as a function of the elongation in the water-quenched and air-cooled Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr g alloys. As expected, the strength increased by air cooling. Table 4 shows the hardness and tensile properties of specimens cold rolled at various reduction ratios after annealing at 1 100°C followed by water quenching. Figure 7(b) shows the plots of the tensile strength as a function of the elongation in the as-quenched and cold-rolled Fe–20Mn–Al–C(–5Cr) alloys. In all specimens, the tensile strength as well as the hardness and yield strength drastically increased with cold rolling at room temperature. The 30% cold-rolled Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr g alloy showed specific strength over 230 MPa · cm3 /g with elongation over 10%. Furthermore, the Fe–20Mn–10Al– 1.5C and Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr alloys cold rolled at reduction ratios more than 90% possessed a high hardness of over 650 Hv and a high specific strength of over 300 $\mathrm { M P a } \cdot \mathrm { c m } ^ { 3 } / \mathrm { g } ,$ , although elongation drastically decreased.

caption: Fig. 8. Relationship between specific strength and tensile elongation in various steels.
